title-group.article-title: Predicting polyvascular disease with optical coherence tomography angiography and/or optical coherence tomography

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/05jscf583grid.410736.70000 0001 2204 9268Department of Cardiology of the Second Affiliated Hospital, State Key Laboratory of Frigid Zone Cardiovascular Diseases (SKLFZCD), Harbin Medical University, No. 246 Xuefu Road, Nangang District, Harbin, 150001 China
2https://ror.org/01mv9t934grid.419897.a0000 0004 0369 313XThe Key Laboratory of Myocardial Ischemia, Chinese Ministry of Education, Harbin, 150001 PR China

3https://ror.org/05jscf583grid.410736.70000 0001 2204 9268Department of Ophthalmology of the Second Affiliated Hospital, Harbin Medical University, No. 246 Xuefu Road, Nangang District, Harbin, 150001 China

4https://ror.org/05jscf583grid.410736.70000 0001 2204 9268Cardiovascular Imaging Center of the Second Affiliated Hospital, Harbin Medical University, No. 246 Xuefu Road, Nangang District, Harbin, 150001 China
5https://ror.org/04yxwc698grid.418668.50000 0001 0275 8630Cardiovascular Research Foundation, New York, United States

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Polyvascular disease (PVD), defined as atherosclerosis involving ≥ 2 vascular beds, confers poor clinical outcome but lacks early noninvasive predictors. This study combined multimodal imaging to explore the retinal vessel characteristics and coronary plaque morphology in patients with PVD.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A total of 311 acute coronary syndrome(ACS) patients who underwent coronary angiography(CAG) with optical coherence tomography(OCT) undertook non-contrast enhanced magnetic resonance angiography(NCE-MRA). Optical coherence tomography angiography(OCTA) was performed on 177 patients among them. Finally 238 patients entered OCT analyses, whereas 172 entered OCTA analyses.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: OCT cohort included 199 PVD patients and 39 non- PVD patients. Compared with non-PVD group, the PVD group had older patients (60.17 ± 8.90 vs. 52.10 ± 11.73, p < 0.001), a lower proportion of men (68.3% vs. 84.6%, p = 0.041), and a higher percentage of patients with diabetes (35.2% vs. 17.9%, p = 0.035). OCT analyses showed culprit plaque of PVD group were more likely to be calcified plaques (13.6% vs. 0%, *p*< 0.05) and had a higher proportion of nodular calcifications (40.7% vs. 17.9%, p = 0.007). OCTA cohort consisted of 137 PVD patients and 35 non- PVD patients. OCTA analyses demonstrated significantly reduced vessel density across all 3 retinal capillary plexuses in PVD group. Multivariate analyses showed that vessel density of full retina fovea was independently predictive of PVD(OR: 0.883, 95% CI: 0.807–0.966, p = 0.007). ROC analysis demonstrated that full retinal vascular density fovea alone yielded an AUC of 0.667 (95% CI: 0.577–0.7556) for predicting PVD.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Full retinal vessel density can serve as a non-invasive predictor for PVD.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12872-026-05650-x.

institution-wrap.institution: National Natural Science Foundation of China

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Polyvascular disease (PVD), defined as atherosclerosis involving ≥ 2 vascular beds (e.g., coronary, carotid, renal, lower extremity, upper mesenteric) [1, 2], is also termed panvascular or multisite artery disease [3]. Its prevalence has risen markedly in recent years [4], conferring substantially higher cardiovascular mortality and morbidity than the general population [5]. Classified as very high-risk [6], PVD remains however frequently underdiagnosed and undertreated [7–9]. Given shared risk factors with coronary artery disease (CAD) [9], holistic management is essential for optimizing outcomes [10]; however, early diagnosis is hindered by atypical presentations and limited awareness [1, 2].

Emerging evidence indicates that microvascular dysfunction precedes macrovascular events [11, 12]. The retina—a highly vascularized tissue whose feature is accessible via optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA)—shares angiogenic signaling pathways with coronary arteries [13, 14]and may reflect systemic endothelial health. Concurrently, coronary optical coherence tomography (OCT) and intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) enable high-resolution plaque characterization. Yet systemic microvascular dysfunction (a potential PVD marker) remains unexplored.

We hypothesize that acute coronary syndrome (ACS) patients with PVD exhibit distinct retinal microvascular alterations on OCTA and high-risk coronary plaque features on OCT/IVUS compared to ACS patients without PVD.

sec.title: Study population
sec.p: This retrospective single-center observational study enrolled 311 patients with acute coronary syndrome (ACS) who underwent coronary angiography (CAG) and OCT/IVUS or OCT between October 2023 and June 2025 at the Second Affiliated Hospital of Harbin Medical University. These patients also consented to undergo non-contrast enhanced magnetic resonance angiography (NCE-MRA) to screen for PVD in the intracranial, carotid, and renal arteries along with OCTA. After excluding 73 cases for various reasons (poor OCT image quality, IVUS-only pullback, unsuitable lesion location, in-stent restenosis, or post-stent imaging), 238 culprit lesions remained analyzable by OCT. Due to an unforeseen termination of the equipment leasing agreement by the manufacturer in September 2024, OCTA imaging was completed in only 177 participants, with 5 excluded due to poor image quality. Figure 1 showed study flowchart. The angiographic criteria of ACS was defined as ≥ 50% diameter stenosis in at least one major epicardial vessel on CAG. Detailed definition of ACS was provided in Supplemental Materials. PVD was identified by NCE-MRA-detected stenosis ≥ 50% in at least one of the aforementioned arteries. Detailed methodology of arterial stenosis assessment was provided in Supplemental Materials. Culprit lesions were determined based on CAG, electrocardiogram, and echocardiography findings. In patients with multiple stenoses, the culprit lesion was identified as follows: lesion with the most severe stenosis or evidence of recent plaque disruption suggestive of thrombus on OCT.

caption.p: Study FlowchartCAG= coronary angiography; OCT= optical coherence tomography; NCE-MRA= non- contrast enhanced magnetic resonance angiography; OCTA= optical coherence tomography angiography; PVD= polyvascular disease; ISR= intrastent restenosis

sec.title: CAG analyses
sec.p: Two independent investigators, blinded to clinical data, performed all analyses using the Coronary Angiography Analysis System (Pie Medical Imaging BV). Culprit and non-culprit lesion distributions were qualitatively assessed. Quantitative measurements included lesion length (LL), minimal lumen diameter (MLD), reference lumen diameter (RLD), and percentage diameter stenosis (DS%). Lesions were classified as Type B2/C and non- Type B2/C, as defined in Supplemental Materials.

sec.title: OCT/IVUS or OCT image acquisition and analyses
sec.p: OCT was performed as needed to guide PCI. Imaging utilized an IVUS-OCT integrated system (Panovision; 304 patients) or a frequency-domain OCT system (ILUMIEN OPTIS; 7 patients). All analyses were conducted at the Intravascular Imaging and Physiology Core Laboratory using Panovision and ILUMIEN OPTIS offline software, respectively. Detailed methodology is provided in the Supplemental Materials (see also Supplemental Fig. 1 for representative images). Quality control procedures and inter- observer agreement were detailed in the Supplemental Materials.

sec.title: NCE-MRA image acquisition and analyses
sec.p: All imaging was performed on a 3.0T Ingenia CX system (Philips) using the following protocols: time-of-flight MRA (TOF-MRA) for intracranial arteries, T1-VISTA for carotid arteries, and balanced turbo field echo MRA (BTF-MRA) for renal arteries. Two independent analysts evaluated all datasets for stenosis, anatomical variations, developmental anomalies, and carotid lipid plaque using standardized protocols on IntelliSpace Portal software (Philips). When there was discordance between the analysts, a consensus reading was obtained from a third analyst. A representative case is shown in Fig. 2. Inter- observer agreement was detailed in the Supplemental Materials.

caption.p: Representative MRA of PVD Case. ACS patient with PVD showed stenosis in intracranial artery (A) and carotid artery (B), with a non-stenotic renal artery (C). Arrow and stars indicate the locations of stenosis. Abbreviations as in Fig. 1

sec.title: OCTA image acquisition and analyses
sec.p: All participants were imaged with an swept source optical coherence tomography angiography(SS-OCTA)system (TowardPi Medical Technology Co., Ltd), acquiring 12 mm × 12 mm macular and 6 mm × 6 mm optic disc scans. An integrated AI algorithm automatically detected retinal/choroidal boundaries for 3D reconstruction and quantitative analysis of the retinal capillary plexuses and choroidal vessels. Following quality control and manual segmentation correction by a masked technician, parameters including vessel density (retinal/choroidal), foveal avascular zone(FAZ) metrics [area, perimeter, flow density in a 300 μm annulus region around FAZ༈FAZ300༉], and thickness (retinal/choroidal, by layer) were analyzed. Poor-quality images were excluded. Detailed parameter definitions are provided in the Supplemental Material, with a representative case in Fig. 3. Intra- observer agreement was detailed in the Supplemental Materials.

caption.p: Representative OCTA Images. Representative swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCTA) images obtained from a non-PVD patient with a scan area of 12 × 12 mm. A B- scan of full retina(area between green lines); B En- face image showing full retinal vessel density with Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) areas. The most inner circle accounts for the fovea area. C En-face image showing the full retinal foveal avascular zone (FAZ) that was outlined automatically(yellow lines). FAZ parameters were calculated automatically and shown in the right lower corner. Abbreviations as in Fig. 1

sec.title: Statistical analyses
sec.p: Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS 25.0, GraphPad Prism 10.4.1 and R 4.4.1. Categorical variables are expressed as frequencies (%) and compared with χ² or Fisher’s exact test. Post-hoc analyses used adjusted standardized residuals (ASR > 1.96 indicating significance after Bonferroni correction). Continuous variables were assessed for normality with Kolmogorov–Smirnov test; normally distributed variables reported as mean ± SD (Student’s t-test or ANOVA) and non-normal as median [interquartile range(IQR)] (Mann–Whitney U or Kruskal-Wallis test). Baseline characteristics were compared across cohorts.

Variables with p < 0.1 in univariable analysis were considered for multivariable binary logistic regression. To avoid multicollinearity among correlated variables (e.g., vessel density metrics from adjacent annulus within the same retinal layer), one clinically representative indicator per cluster was selected. The OCTA predicting PVD model included two predictors and was evaluated with likelihood ratio tests and variance inflation factors (VIF) (all < 2.0).

For complete separation (e.g., calcified plaque absent in non-PVD group), Firth’s penalized regression was applied (logistf package in R). Results from both conventional logistic regression (for non-separated variables) and Firth regression (for separated variables) are collectively presented in one table. ROC analysis (AUC with 95% CI) was performed using R (for model containing separated variables) or GraphPad Prism 10.4.1(for model not containing separated variables); DeLong’s test compared correlated ROC curves. OCTA parameters were averaged between eyes. The potential implications of this averaging, including the assumption of broadly similar bilateral involvement and the reduction of inter-eye correlation, are acknowledged. Inter- observer reliability was evaluated using the kappa coefficient for categorical parameters and Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC)for continuous parameters. All tests were two-tailed with p < 0.05 considered significant.

sec.title: Baseline and procedural characteristics
sec.p: According to results from NCE-MRA, 199 of the 238 patients had polyvascular disease (PVD), whereas 39 were not found to have PVD. A few differences of baseline characteristics were detected between PVD and non- PVD groups, including that patients in PVD group were older(60.17 ± 8.90 years vs. 52.10 ± 11.73 years; p<0.001), less likely to be male(68.3% vs. 84.6%; p = 0.041), had more prevalent DM(35.2% vs. 17.9%; p = 0.035) (Table 1). Distribution of lipid plaque in carotid artery were also different as presented in Table 1 between two groups(χ2(2) = 22.48, p < 0.001). Specifically, the PVD group exhibited a higher prevalence of both unilateral plaques (37.4% vs. 12.8%) (Adj. std. residual = + 3.0, *p* < 0.01) and bilateral plaques (26.8% vs. 10.3%) (Adj. std. residual = + 2.2, *p* < 0.05). Procedural characteristics showed no difference between two groups.

caption.p: Baseline and procedural characteristics of ACS patients with and without PVD

table-wrap-foot.p: Values are mean ± SD, n (%), or median (IQR). A P value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant, shown in bold

BMI Body mass index, CKD Chronic kidney disease, HbA1c Glycated hemoglobin, HDL-C High-density lipoprotein cholesterol, LDL-C Low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, LPa Lipoprotein a, LVEF Left ventricular ejection fraction, NCE-MRA Non-contrast enhanced magnetic resonance angiography, NSTEMI Non–ST-segment elevated myocardial infarction, PCI Percutaneous coronary intervention, STEMI ST-segment elevated myocardial infarction, UAP Unstable angina pectoris
*Adjusted std. residual > |1.96| (*p* < 0.05 after Bonferroni correction for 6 cells)

sec.title: Angiographic and OCT characteristics of culprit lesions
sec.p: 98 patients underwent one- vessel optical coherence tomography (OCT) scanning, 87 underwent two- vessel scanning while 53 underwent three- vessel OCT imaging. Table 2 showed the characteristics of coronary angiography (CAG) and OCT in culprit lesions. Plaque types were distinguished between two groups(p = 0.022), amongst ACS patients with PVD showed higher prevalence of calcified plaque type (13.6% vs. 0%) (Adj. std. residual = + 2.4, *p* < 0.05). Moreover, PVD group also exhibited more frequent nodular calcification (40.7% vs. 17.9%, p = 0.007). The baseline characteristics and culprit lesion features were taken into univariate logistic regression and Firth logistic regression analyses to assess the independent predictors of PVD (Supplemental Table 1). All variables significant in univariate analyses (p < 0.1) in addition to clinically important factors (e.g., hypertension, BMI, smoking, LDL) were included in the multivariable model(Supplemental Table 1). At multivariate analyses, unilateral carotid lipid plaque (OR: 5.420; 95% CI: 2.032–16.89.3; p < 0.001), and bilateral carotid lipid plaque (OR: 5.698; 95% CI: 1.771–24.108; p = 0.002) were positively associated with the clinical presentation of PVD. The multivariate Firth logistic regression model incorporating the following variables [age, sex(reference: male), BMI, HTN, DM, smoking, LDL, carotid plaque status(reference: none), calcified nodule, plaque rupture(reference: others), calcified plaque(reference: lipidic plaque)] was significantly predictive of PVD. The model’s discriminatory power, as assessed by ROC analysis, showed area under the curve (AUC) of 0.837 (95% CI: 0.758–0.916), representing good discrimination(Supplemental Fig. 2a). No difference was detected in the angiographic characteristics of non-culprit lesions (Supplemental Table 2).

caption.p: Angiographic and OCT findings of culprit lesions

table-wrap-foot.p: Values are n (%), mean ± SD, or median (IQR). A P value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant, shown in bold

DS Diameter stenosis, FCT Fibrous cap thickness, LAD Left anterior descending artery, LCX Left circumflex artery, MLA Minimal lumen area, MLD Minimal lumen diameter, OCT Optical coherence tomography, RCA Right coronary artery, RLA Reference lumen area, RV/LD Reference vessel/lumen diameter, TCFA Thin-cap fibroatheroma; other abbreviations as in Table 1
*Adjusted std. residual > |1.96| (*p* < 0.05 after Bonferroni correction for 6 cells)

sec.title: OCTA characteristics
sec.p: NCE-MRA found 137 PVD patients and 35 non- PVD patients in the optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) cohort. Table 3 showed the eye- level comparison of retinal characteristics between PVD group and non- PVD group. Quantitative OCTA analysis revealed significant microvascular alterations in PVD patients compared to non-PVD controls. The PVD group exhibited significantly reduced FAZ300 parameters in full[37.82(32.08–41.23)% vs. 40.20(35.88–42.11)%; p = 0.024], superficial [39.01(32.69–42.22)% vs. 40.27(36.29–42.77)%; p = 0.018], and deep retina [39.46(34.49–41.75)% vs. 40.82(38.70–43.34.70.34)%; p < 0.001]. Concurrently, increased retinal central foveal thickness was observed in PVD patients (257.40 ± 28.57 μm vs. 249.49 ± 18.14 μm; p = 0.005), as well as elevated full retinal thickness(302.96 ± 25.56 μm vs. 295.30 ± 22.48 μm, p = 0.024). Retinal nerve fiber layer(RNFL) perfusion was compromised in the PVD group, with significantly lower mean blood flow [47.61(46.34–48.41)% vs. 47.99༈47.31–48.58༉; p = 0.038]. Vessel density analysis using the ETDRS grid demonstrated reduced perfusion across all retinal vascular layers in PVD patients as shown in Table 3. No significant intergroup differences were observed in superficial retinal thickness, deep retinal thickness, choroidal thickness, or choroidal vessel density. These null findings are detailed in Supplemental Table 3. The OCTA characteristics were taken into univariate logistic regression to assess the independent predictors of PVD (Supplemental Table 4). All variables significant in univariate analyses (p < 0.1) in addition to clinically relevant factors (e.g., hypertension, BMI, smoking, LDL) were included in the multivariable model(Supplemental Table 4). At multivariate analyses, full retinal vessel density fovea, (OR: 0.883; 95% CI: 0.807–0.966; p = 0.007), was negatively associated with the clinical presentation of PVD. In the assessment of diagnostic performance for predicting PVD, the full retinal vessel density fovea alone demonstrated an AUC of 0.667(95% CI: 0.577–0.756)(Fig. 4a). A multivariable model incorporating this parameter with established clinical risk factors (e.g., age, HTN, smoking and DM) showed significantly improved discriminatory power, with an AUC of 0.841(95% CI: 0.775–0.907) (Fig. 4b). This represented a significant incremental value over a model containing only the clinical risk factors (AUC = 0.778, 95% CI: 0.692–0.865), with a difference in AUC of 0.063 (DeLong’s test p = 0.035).

caption.p: Retinal OCTA Characteristics of ACS Patients with and without PVD (eye based)

table-wrap-foot.p: Values are n (%), mean ± SD, or median (IQR). A P value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant, shown in bold

DCP Deep capillary plexus, FAZ Foveal avascular zone, FAZ300 Flow density in a 300 μm annulus region around FAZ, RNFL Retinal nerve fiber layer, SCP Superficial capillary plexus, other abbreviations as in Table 1

caption.p: Performance of Full Retinal Vessel Density Fovea Predicting PVD. Receiver operating characteristic(ROC) curves of full retinal vessel density fovea alone A showed area under the curve(AUC) of 0.667, 95% CI: 0.577–0.7556. B showed incremental value of AUC provided by full retinal vessel density fovea: AUC of clinical factors incorporating full retinal vessel density fovea is 0.841(95% CI: 0.775–0.907), while that of clinical factors alone is 0.778(95% CI: 0.692–0.865). The difference is 0.063 (DeLong’s test p = 0.035). The axes, representing sensitivity and 1 - specificity, are scaled in percentage terms. CI= confidence interval. Abbreviations as in Fig. 1

sec.title: OCTA and OCT combined to predict PVD
sec.p: Excluding unanalyzable OCT and OCTA, 106 patients had OCT and OCTA. A multivariate Firth logistic regression model incorporating the following variables (age, sex(reference: male), BMI, HTN, DM, smoking, LDL, carotid plaque status(reference: none), calcified nodule, plaque rupture(reference: others), calcified plaque(reference: lipidic plaque), full retinal vessel density fovea) was significantly predictive of PVD. The model’s discriminatory power, as assessed by ROC analysis, is presented in Supplemental Fig. 2b. The AUC was 0.869 (95% CI: 0.773–0.965), representing excellent discrimination.

sec.title: Key findings
sec.p: This is the first multi-modality imaging study looking into features of ACS with PVD, revealing that PVD group had more calcified plaque and nodular calcification, reduced retinal vessel density, more prevalent unilateral and bilateral lipid plaque in carotid artery. Full retinal vessel density fovea was able to independently predict PVD.

sec.title: Clinical factors and PVD
sec.p: The prevalence of PVD (83.6%) in our cohort is higher than in general population and previous trials. It stems from our study design: enrollment of ACS patients (inherently high-risk with established coronary disease) combined with systematic NCE-MRA screening of carotid, intracranial, and renal territories. This imaging-based ascertainment uncovered extensive subclinical disease—consistent with literature demonstrating that symptom-driven diagnosis significantly underestimates true PVD burden [7–9]. This finding empirically validates PVD’s frequent underdiagnosis and underscores the clinical imperative for proactive multisite vascular assessment in ACS.

Compared to non-PVD patients, those with PVD were older, had higher rates of diabetes, and more frequent unilateral/bilateral carotid lipid-rich plaques. The link with age aligns with evidence that aging promotes multi-territory atherosclerosis [15]. The pronounced diabetic burden is consistent with its known role in endothelial dysfunction and plaque progression [16, 17].

Notably, the higher proportion of women with PVD may seem contrary to population studies showing minimal sex differences [18]. This discrepancy likely arises because our study included only ACS patients—a high-risk group in which women may lose typical cardiovascular protection due to risk factor accumulation, sex-specific conditions (e.g., autoimmune disease, adverse pregnancy outcomes), and management disparities [19]. This pattern mirrors the elevated CAD risk among women with diabetes, potentially reflecting shared pathways such as endothelial inflammation and microvascular dysfunction [20]. These findings emphasize the need for tailored secondary prevention in high-risk women, including systematic screening for PVD and earlier aggressive therapy, as also highlighted by Cho et al [21].

Furthermore, PVD patients showed significantly more bilateral carotid plaque, indicating systemic atherosclerosis [22]. Bilateral involvement suggests advanced disease and underscores the need for comprehensive vascular assessment in high-risk patients to improve risk stratification and guide intensive strategies.

sec.title: OCT and PVD
sec.p: OCT analysis revealed that PVD patients exhibited a calcification-predominant phenotype at culprit lesions, with significantly higher rates of calcified plaques and nodular calcification, but fewer lipid plaques. We therefore interpret this phenotype as a marker of advanced, chronic systemic atherosclerotic burden rather than acute plaque instability—a distinction critical for contextualizing coronary findings within the broader pathophysiology of polyvascular disease. This pattern aligns with coronary artery calcium (CAC) scores as an established predictor of panvascular risk [23]. The prominence of nodular calcification suggests accelerated calcific transformation, supporting its link to adverse cardiovascular outcomes as demonstrated in broader populations [24]. These OCT-based calcification features may reflect underlying systemic vulnerability and represent in situ biomarkers of progressive calcific atherosclerosis in PVD.

sec.title: OCTA and PVD
sec.p: In clinical practice, reduced retinal vessel density could serve as an accessible, non-invasive indicator prompting targeted multi-territory evaluation (e.g., carotid ultrasound, ABI) in ACS patients, particularly when coronary imaging reveals highly calcified features. This approach leverages OCTA’s practical advantages (rapid, radiation-free, low-cost) while acknowledging its role as an adjunct—not a replacement—for established vascular assessments.

The reduced vessel density, particularly in the metabolically active DCP susceptible to ischemia [25–28], suggests generalized microvascular rarefaction and impaired perfusion, mirroring the endothelial dysfunction underlying panvascular disease. These findings align with Zhang et al. [26], Cao et al. [29] and Li et al. [30], who identified parafoveal vessel density loss as a systemic vascular risk marker. Klein et al. [31] also linked retinal arteriolar abnormalities (assessed by fundus photography) to atherosclerotic diseases, corroborating our findings. Mihály et al. [32] further reported that a compromised Circle of Willis reduced retinal vessel density in carotid disease, consistent with our results.

FAZ enlargement and morphological changes further indicate capillary integrity loss and remodeling from chronic hypoperfusion and inflammatory injury [25, 33], aligning with Duan et al. [34] who correlated FAZ changes with ischemic stroke severity.

When integrated with coronary OCT/IVUS, retinal microvascular metrics provide incremental pathophysiological insight—linking systemic microvascular dysfunction to focal macrovascular vulnerability. This ‘micro-macro vascular integration’ enhances holistic risk assessment in ACS patients, aligning with current guidelines advocating comprehensive vascular evaluation in high-risk cohorts. A non-invasive OCTA scan can quantify systemic vascular burden, reflecting the recognition that microvascular disease drives macrovascular events [35–37]. As an embryological extension of cerebral circulation [38], the retina provides a unique observable microvascular network; its changes likely parallel damage in heart, brain, and kidneys, explaining its link to multi-territory atherosclerosis. This pattern, consistent with Wang et al. [39] reporting vessel density decrease in CAD, suggests retinal vessel density assessment could improve risk stratification to identify high-risk patients with subclinical PVD for aggressive prevention. Prospective validation is needed to define quantitative thresholds and confirm whether adding retinal metrics to coronary imaging significantly improves prediction of polyvascular involvement or hard cardiovascular outcomes beyond conventional risk stratification.

While the combined OCT–OCTA analysis was exploratory and conducted in a selected subgroup with available multimodal imaging, its robust discriminatory performance provides a compelling proof-of-concept for integrated microvascular–macrovascular phenotyping in polyvascular disease. These preliminary findings warrant prospective validation in larger, independent cohorts to confirm clinical utility—without diminishing the novelty and pathophysiological insight offered by this multimodal approach.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This study has several important limitations. First, its retrospective and observational design inherently introduces unmeasured confounding and selection bias. Second, as a single-center experience, the generalizability may be limited. Third, due to circumstances beyond control, only part of the patients completed OCTA. Nevertheless, OCTA availability is independent of patient characteristics which means missing was completely at random. This brought in a potential limitation that the independent predictor from the OCTA subset and the ROC analysis derived from the OCT- OCTA combined cohort may not be directly generalizable to the entire study population. However baseline characteristics were comparable across successive cohorts (initial n = 311; OCT n = 238; OCTA n = 177; combined n = 106; Supplemental Table 5), minimizing selection bias. Fourth, the systematic assessment of non-culprit lesions was limited, as 3-vessel OCT was acquired in a small, non-random portion of the population. This was mostly due to clinical constraints and safety considerations. Therefore, our analysis is necessarily confined to the culprit lesion and cannot speak to the pan-coronary plaque burden or the vulnerability of non-culprit lesions. Fifth, a significant limitation of this study lies in the anatomical coverage of the NCE-MRA by focusing solely on the intracranial, carotid, and renal arteries. This incomplete assessment introduces a potential for misclassification bias. Thus, our results and conclusions are specifically relevant to the cohort of patients with ACS who also have evidence of atherosclerosis in one or more of these three specific vascular beds.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: OCTA and OCT provide a multimodal assessment of PVD’s microvascular and plaque features, meriting prospective validation. Our study concludes that reduced retinal vessel density on OCTA is a robust, independent predictor of PVD in ACS patients. Integrating this non-invasive biomarker with structural OCT and clinical factors significantly improves risk stratification for extensive atherosclerosis, positioning the eye as a window to systemic vasculopathy. This rapid, accessible tool can guide diagnostics and therapy, warranting future large-scale studies to validate its role in panvascular risk assessment.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We are grateful to the staff of the Myocardial Ischemia Key Laboratory for their help and support. We thank the staff at Home for Researchers for their assistance in preparing the graphical abstract for this manuscript.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: Conceptualization: H.J., B.Y., S.H., C.Z. and P.F. Methodology: S.O., B. Z., M.L, T.W. and Q.S. Validation: D.Z., H.R., N.W., S.Z and X.L. Investigation: X.L., S.L., X.G., Y, L., X.Z., P.F. and X.Z. Resources: Q.S., S.H., M.L., P.F, and S.Z. Writing-Original Draft: M.L. Writing-Review & Editing: G.M., H.J., S.Z., X. L. Funding Acquisition: S.H., H.J. Supervision: H.J. and B.Y.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation Projects from Dr Jia (No.82370464), he also received Key Research and Development Program of Heilongjiang Provincial Science and Technology Department (2024ZX02D0094, 2024ZX02C30). Also Dr. Hu received a grant from National Natural Science Foundation of China (82470341), Key Research and Development Program of Heilongjiang Provincial Science and Technology Department (2024ZX12C01), and Fund of the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Provincial Science and Technology Department (PL2024H085). Dr. Li received Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Fund to Pursue Scientific Research(LBH-722209). Dr. Luo received Postdoctoral Research Funding of Heilongjiang Province (LBH-Z24219), China Postdoctoral Funding Project (2025M772156) and Postdoctoral Innovation Talent Support Program (BX20250260).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The datasets used and/or analysed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study was conducted in accordance with the ethical principles of the Declaration of Helsinki. The study was approved by the ethics committee of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Harbin Medical University, and all participants provided written informed consent.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

element-citation.article-title: Polyvascular disease: reappraisal of the current clinical landscape

element-citation.article-title: Epidemiology of peripheral artery disease and polyvascular disease
